comments.text: This is another example of academic hype being marketed via popular science. There are far more established and proven pedagogical approaches (like sustained project-based learning or differentiated reading instruction) than there are 'breakthrough' single-focus tech solutions. Counting all the methods with long-term, proven efficacy, we’re sitting at 19 decades of research versus 1 shiny new algorithm. I think the concept of 'AI-driven gaps' is just an easy way into the mainstream conference circuit, but universal design principles are the real future. It just didn't get the same grant funding cycle, for whatever reason.

replies.text: Bingo! The advantage of AI modules is that they are incredibly easy to describe to a parent or administrator, giving them massive PR value during accreditation visits.
replies.original_text: Bingo! CRISPR has an advantage of being relatively easy to describe to a layman, giving it a PR advantage.

comments.original_text: CRISPR is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular science. There is 1 FDA approved CRISPR therapy as compared to 7 for AAV and 7 for Lentivirus. Counting all viral vector therapies that have been approved, we’re sitting at 19 approved therapies versus 1 for CRISPR. I think CRISPR ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but viral vector delivery is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

comments.text: I hope this finally works out. I remember almost exactly ten years ago I got excited about one of these proposed 'silver bullet' curriculum reforms, tried to talk about it at a staff meeting, and they laughed at me for believing.

replies.text: I'm pretty optimistic. I think educational progress is a threshold question where we need a number of basic, foundational skills to all get over certain bars before the floodgates start to open. Over the past 1-2 decades there has been unbelievable progress in blended learning tech, but most people are unimpressed because they haven't translated into systemic reform yet due to not individually being sufficient to cause an explosion of progress. IMO, we're finally seeing it as so many different technologies have gotten so cheap, fast, and accessible.

replies.text: Real, longitudinal student development isn't going away and will indeed be a powerful tool to face learning gaps. It is not the same story as any one particular piece of EdTech fizzling out to nothing, it is a class of tools (AI, blended learning, etc.) that is maturing into the realm of early curricular deployment.

comments.text: Obviously this is a personal pedagogical preference, but the multiple layers of 'Bloom's Taxonomy' bullet points on the slide deck is not something I like, as it forces a lot of grouping on the eye where it doesn't serve any purpose. comments.text: My first thought is how redundant these lesson plans are. I used to work in curriculum development, and this was often the case. Curricula are always filled to the rafters. The only way to fit new standards in is to retire old standards. If they didn't, the department would only...

comments.text: "I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the variability of student performance on standardized assessments (the 95th percentile range) is higher than the district average suggests. replies.text: The component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe interop between modules written in different languages (i.e., different departments). Given that the school is a hub for many curricula, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose skills in a language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core pedagogy with the global framework. There is some overlap in people, but the process is separate.
replies.original_text: The component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe interop between modules written in different languages. Given that Wasm is a runtime target for many languages, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose APIs in an language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core WebAssembly work with the WASI work. There is some overlap in people, but WASI is developed separately.

comments.text: People need to get into their heads that the 'Curriculum Underground' (CU) is just a collection of user-produced lesson plans and resources. You have to review the source of every resource/PKGBUILD from the CU you install, full stop. Yes that includes any updates. This really has always been the case; we've had discussion about this for well over a decade. People are always asking why there's no official, automated resource checker like 'yay'—this is why. A lot of people complain about education being elitist, but the simple reality is it's a field built for people who know what they are doing and don't need or want their hand held at every step of the...

replies.text: Regardless of it being just a collection of user-produced lesson plans, the department would certainly benefit from a more robust solution to this issue. Expecting users to manually review every single resource, for every single unit they are using, every single time they update or install a new module is just unreasonable if you want the CU to be useful at all for the general practitioner teacher.

comments.text: The current EdTech landscape is weird. Developing nations or rural schools often rely on basic communication methods (like WhatsApp/SMS) because centralized, high-fidelity systems are too costly. You're trying to monetize against institutions serving resource-constrained populations. Yes, they might be in higher number, but the required infrastructure margins are razor thin. Eventually, the major providers will just settle on mandatory email and basic Google Classroom/Teams integration.
comments.original_text: WhatsApp business api is in a weird situation. the poor countries use WhatsApp mostly due the fact that sms was costly. so you're tryna to monetize against businesses serving poorer users. yes - they maybe more in number but margins are razor thin. the richer countries where margins are higher - sms, email, etc are cheaper & permission less. so eventually most providers most settling on email & sms/rcs.

comments.text: It's genuinely difficult to get programmatic access to a reliable student communication service. Google Classroom is the only one that seems to make it easy, but it assumes you're a well-funded institution with 1:M communication needs. Canvas and Schoology have first-party solutions that assume perfect data compliance. Signal (or basic email) doesn't want complex API users at all. Maybe I need to start looking at old-school mailing lists, but that means giving up all the modern features and user experience.
comments.original_text: It's weird how hard it is to get programmatic access to a mobile-first messaging service. Telegram is the only one that makes it easy. Maybe I need to start looking at SMS/MMS proper, but that's putting a lot of information in the clear and giving up a lot of features.

comments.text: "Don't expend more effort than they are" has actually long been a good principle to have internalized in the classroom. Someone who submits a paper with only superficial research and asks a basic question during office hours? Give a cursory, guiding answer. Someone who obviously spent hours drafting a deep comparative analysis? Give them a significant chunk of your time. Someone who only asks vague questions on the LMS? Either don't respond, or respond with a guiding prompt. Someone who clearly engaged with the core concepts and took time to refine their position? Take time to engage with their ideas too.

replies.text: I've had this same policy since we started using IB assessments. I formalized it for myself (and this department) after enough instances of 'I'm trying to analyze X. It's not working. Help.' type emails. You need to put as much effort into the pre-work and scaffolding of the question as you expect someone to put into the answer. It's not about 'fairness' or 'AI' or anything else, it's that doing this any other way fundamentally breaks the academic dynamic. You have a problem. You want a grade. If the cost to you is effectively nil, but the cost to the educator is non-zero, we have a serious pedagogical problem.

replies.text: As someone who graded ~4x the median number of drafts on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is the 'draft' as a concept. Grading doesn't scale to prolific submissions, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on draft-based assessment for human students (robust meta-cognition requirements, auto-graders, comprehensive end-to-end portfolio assessments, etc.), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.
replies.original_text: As someone who pushed ~4x the median PRs on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is PRs as a concept. Code review doesn't scale to prolific humans, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on PRs for human developers (auto-formatters, linters, comprehensive end-to-end tests, continuous deployment pipelines, etc), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.

comments.text: I will always have a soft spot for the original IB curriculum structure. It was one of those methods I taught constantly as a child, although only when the department head would let us use the old syllabus materials. I only realize it now but it had some very unique pedagogical mechanics that even today you don't see very often (ok maybe that's a bit of a stretch but the mechanics were novel to me back then): - Notably, you had to write a full research paper for the Theory of Knowledge component. Failing the TOK essay didn't reset your final grade, so there was constant pressure to keep analyzing the sources. - There is a satisfying cross-curricular link requirement. This is still rare to see in secondary assessment. - Incredibly...

It was my 5 minutes of fame at my computer class when I arrived and showed that I had passed the literature seminar...

comments.text: The department heads of curriculum still talking about the assessment criteria more than 30 years later seems very bizarre to me. replies.text: The curriculum came out in 1989, while Inquiry-Based Learning arrived in 1992. In the late 80s and early 90s, educational pedagogy was evolving at a breakneck pace, a 3-year gap was an eternity. Skills-based teaching ran quite well on an 8-bit XT, while modern constructivism needed a 16-bit 286. They are basically from a different era, and Inquiry-Based Learning was surely inspired by the Core Curriculum.

replies.text: Curriculum boards do regulate core components, and accrediting bodies regulate what claims they can make. It's hard to make a binary decision that one assessment model is 'good' and another isn't. You can appreciate there is a spectrum of student needs with gifted learners and remedial support in the middle, and gap-filling workshops on the other end. Personally, I think that school districts simply don't treat 'student readiness' as seriously as they should, and so the market has not provided more robust, standardized solutions.
replies.original_text: FDA does regulate cosmetics components, and FTC regulates what claims they can make. It’s hard to make a binary decision that one thing is a drug and something else isn’t. comments.text: There are a lot of things like this. My favorite is how elegant pedagogical solutions often look simple in retrospect. So if you noodle on a curriculum unit for a while and then come up with a clever assessment method: once you explain it to a parent or principal they'll be like, 'yeah, of course.' Meanwhile, the colleague next to you that overcomplicates the problem with excessive scaffolding ends up getting kudos for building something so difficult. :D

replies.text: Yep. Administrators perceive complexity by how personally confused they are. I'm late in my career, and I'm realizing I wasted so many man-hours trying to make a unit globally integrated, culturally responsive, and maintainable, when that curriculum was never read by another person again and forgotten 15 minutes after the accreditation visit. This is why I think 'good enough' is fine, and keeps the school running.
replies.original_text: Yeap. Managers perceive complexity by how personally confused they are. I'm late in my career, and I'm realizing I wasted so many man years trying to make code clean, user friendly, and maintainable, when that code was never read by another person again and forgotten 15 minutes after it was released. This is why I think AI is coming for our jobs much sooner than many people think: clean code, separation of concerns, maintainability, etc, all the things we spend the most time on, have never actually been valued. "Good enough" is fine, and keeps management happy.

stories.title: Assessment practices succumbing to Standardized Testing: more than just a curriculum adjustment (2025)

comments.text: Rote testing inherently sacrifices the depth of inquiry gained through project-based learning (PBL). The 'warm feel' of genuine student engagement is lost when every unit must be boiled down to a multiple-choice bubble sheet. High-stakes assessment is necessary, but the constant need for quantifiable metrics inevitably leads to curricular compression.

replies.text: Which is why it's so baffling that internal assessments are often judged against the same narrow metrics as external exams. We are trained to see the holistic learner, but the system only grades the standardized output.
replies.original_text: Which is why it's so bizarre that CDs are generally less dynamic than vinyl. There's no technical reason that should be the case.

replies.text: > We also see this with college applications. Students who thrive in complex, open-ended discussions are penalized by the need to select only the 'most appropriate' extracurricular bullet point.
replies.original_text: > Vinyl literally has less than half the dynamic range of CD A lot less than half. It's around 20-30db and every 10db is a factor of 10. The CD has between 100-1000x more dynamic range.

comments.original_text: Vinyl literally has less than half the dynamic range of CD and has always been compressed before cutting. Hard limiting is a (stupid) choice, but some limiting has always been necessary. The "warm vinyl sound" is basically analog compression with added low-end distortion from the RIAA compensation and some wrinkles at the high end caused by stylus resonance.

comments.text: Also covered by the IB World School Review (2025) -- You need to be careful when adopting new assessment models— the pressure for quick, measurable data can make pedagogically sound methods look worse than outdated, inefficient ones: https://www.schoolreview.edu/assessment/new-models-and-the-curriculum-shift...

comments.text: I thought the assessment reform wave was over? This was a hot topic for many years in the academic community. Sad to see it still persists. Especially since the answer to any pedagogical problem is to simply for the user to spend more time on it.

replies.text: The focus on 'student agency' in the strict sense of 'we must let students decide everything to make learning good' is over thanks to standardized testing doing competency mapping, but there's still an open question of 'how much freedom does a curriculum need?' which is extremely subjective. A lot of modern curriculum is looser than peak mandated core standards just because the administrators want it to be maximally flexible. I recommend using MYP units and -3, -2, -1 standards are common.
replies.original_text: The loudness war in the strict "we must compress and slam everything as much as possible to make our stuff sound good on the radio" sense is over thanks to Spotify et al doing loudness normalization, but there's still an open question of "how much compression does sound good?" which is extremely subjective. A lot of modern music is louder than peak loudness war stuff just because the creators want their music to be slammed to shit. I listen to/make aggressive bass music and -5, -4, -3 LUFS are common.
